title: Counting Belugas From Orbit
lines: Under the gray silt of Cook Inlet swims one of the rarest whales on earth, almost invisible.
About three hundred thirty one Cook Inlet belugas are left, down roughly eighty percent since 1979.
So NOAA is trying something new. Find them from space.
A NOAA project called Gaia, built with U S G S, Microsoft, and the Navy.
It reads satellite photos and learns from people, one labeled whale at a time.
But it cannot count belugas yet. It is still learning to see.
And to work, it needs a clear look straight down.
In June 2025, it lined up with the whale survey and came back empty.
The Anchorage sky was likely booked, a busy port and an air base wanting the same slice.
The whales may finally be holding on, and this new eye is real.
The question is whether the sky it needs ever stays open.
synthid_disclosure: Gemini native TTS, preset voice Sulafat, carries a SynthID watermark (not a clone).
honesty_check: ML not 'deep learning'; 331 is an aerial-survey number, never attributed to GAIA; June 2025 cause is NOAA's 'likely'; the tool is experimental, not a deployed counter.
